mc.description: You buy Bitcoin today at {price}. We simulate 2,000 possible outcomes over 3 years using a proprietary model built for how Bitcoin actually behaves — crashes, rallies, and the long silences in between. The result is a probability map of what could happen and how likely each scenario is.
wp.title: Whitepaper
wp.subtitle: A quantitative framework for answering the only question that matters
wp.builtBy: Built by Edu Forte and CommonSense Digital Asset Management. Barcelona.
wp.t01.title: The question
wp.t01.p1: Everyone who looks at Bitcoin eventually asks the same thing: should I buy it now?
wp.t01.p2: The problem isn't information — there's too much of it. Twitter threads, YouTube analysts, on-chain metrics, technical indicators, macro takes. Most of it is noise dressed as signal. And the few tools that try to be rigorous tend to be either too academic to be useful or too simple to be honest.
wp.t01.p3: We wanted to build something different. A model that takes 16 years of daily price data, runs it through institutional-grade quantitative analysis, and gives you one answer: **yes or no**, with your actual odds of losing money at 1 year and 3 years.
wp.t02.title: The Power Law foundation
wp.t02.p1: In 2019, Giovanni Santostasi published an observation that would become one of the most debated ideas in Bitcoin analysis: Bitcoin's price follows a power law. Not approximately — with an R² above 0.95 across the entire history of the asset.
wp.t02.p2: A power law means that when you plot price against time on a log-log scale, you get a straight line. Power laws appear in network effects, city sizes, earthquake magnitudes. They describe systems where growth decelerates as the system matures.
wp.t02.p3: Harold Christopher Burger popularized this into a model: fit an OLS regression in log-log space, draw standard deviation bands around it, and you get a corridor. The bottom is the support floor. The top is the bubble zone. Fair value is the line.
wp.t02.p4: It's an elegant framework. And it's largely correct. But it has three problems.
wp.t03.title: Where we diverge: WLS, RANSAC, EVT
wp.t03.p1: **Problem 1: OLS treats all data equally.** [d]Burger's model gives the same weight to a $1 trade in 2011 and a $70,000 trade in 2024. But these are fundamentally different markets.[/d]
wp.t03.p2: Our fix: **Weighted Least Squares** with exponential decay. We give more weight to recent data using a 4-year half-life. Our slope (b={bValue}) is lower than Burger's (b=5.85). That means our model sees Bitcoin's growth as decelerating — which is what you'd expect from a maturing asset.
wp.t03.p3: **Problem 2: The support floor ignores bubbles.** [d]OLS fits to all data, including bubble peaks. Those peaks pull the regression line up, making the floor unreliable during crashes.[/d]
wp.t03.p4: Our fix: **RANSAC** (Random Sample Consensus). A robust regression that excludes outliers automatically. The RANSAC fit has its own steeper slope, meaning the support envelope has its own structural dynamics.
wp.t03.p5: **Problem 3: The bubble ceiling is arbitrary.** Our fix: **Extreme Value Theory** with a Generalized Pareto Distribution fitted to actual positive residuals above the 85th percentile.
wp.t03.p6: [d]All three changes build on the same Power Law. We're not replacing Santostasi and Burger. We're calibrating their model for today's market.[/d]
wp.t04.title: Why normal models fail Bitcoin
wp.t04.p1: Bitcoin's daily returns have a kurtosis above 10. A normal distribution has a kurtosis of 3. Extreme moves happen 5 to 10 times more often than Gaussian models predict.
wp.t04.p2: [d]This isn't a quirk. It's structural. Bitcoin exhibits fat tails, volatility clustering, and long memory (Hurst exponent H > 0.55).[/d]
wp.t04.p3: Our solution: **Mandelbrot's Multifractal Model of Asset Returns (MMAR)**. Volatility isn't random — it has a self-similar structure across time scales.
wp.t04.p4: [d]We implement MMAR through DFA for the Hurst exponent, multifractal partition function for λ², and regime-switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck for state detection.[/d]
wp.t05.title: 2,000 possible futures
wp.t05.p1: We simulate 2,000 paths over a 3-year horizon. Each path uses fractal cascades, Hurst-correlated noise, empirical shock resampling, and a reflective floor at RANSAC support.
wp.t05.p2: What we deliberately excluded: **the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck mean-reversion force**. [d]Early versions pulled prices back toward fair value. This artificially dampened tail risk. The paths were more honest on pure MMAR/Hurst dynamics.[/d]
wp.t05.p3: From 2,000 paths we extract: percentiles at each horizon, probability of loss at 1 month through 3 years, and probability of reaching fair value.
wp.t06.title: The signal: pure σ, no optimization
wp.t06.p1: The signal is simple. Not because we couldn't build something complex, but because we tested the complex version and it wasn't more accurate — just harder to defend.
wp.t06.p2: [d]σ is the number of standard deviations from Power Law fair value. No neural networks. No optimization.[/d]
wp.t06.p3: Why not optimize? **Because optimization lies.** When you run a grid search over 625 weight combinations and report the best result, you're reporting in-sample accuracy.
wp.t06.p4: [d]We built a robust backtest that refits the Power Law at each point using only historical data. σ < -0.5 works with 100% accuracy even with 8 years of data. The threshold is structural.[/d]
wp.t06.p5: The sell signal at σ ≥ 0.8 requires PL maturity (b > 4.0). The Power Law needs ~8 years before the slope converges.
wp.t07.title: What we know, and what we don't
wp.t07.p1: **What the model gets right:** The buy signal has never been wrong. 100% accuracy across every cycle since 2016. The sell signal: when σ exceeded 0.8, 94% lost money at 12 months.
wp.t07.p2: **What the model gets wrong:** The Monte Carlo systematically underestimates risk above σ = 0.5.
wp.t07.p3: [d]The MC predicts 6-24% loss in elevated zones. Actual: 62-94%. This is why the sell signal uses σ thresholds directly.[/d]
wp.t07.p4: The Power Law is empirical, not physical. Episode analysis uses 5-7 episodes — a tiny sample. The model cannot predict structural breaks.
wp.t07.p5: **This is a probability map — the best one we know how to build. That is not a guarantee.**
faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.a1: The model provides a live signal based on how far Bitcoin's price is from its Power Law fair value. When the signal says 'buy' (σ < -0.5), historically the price was higher 12 months later 100% of the time. Check the Lite tab for today's answer.
faq.a2: Sigma measures how many standard deviations Bitcoin's current price is from its Power Law fair value. Negative sigma = undervalued (buy zone). Positive sigma = overvalued (caution/sell zone). Buy triggers at σ < -0.5, sell at σ > 0.8.
faq.q3: What is the Power Law model?
faq.a3: A mathematical relationship between Bitcoin's price and time: log(price) = a + b × log(days). It has held with R² > 0.9 for 16 years. We use Weighted Least Squares with 4-year decay so recent data matters more.
faq.q4: How accurate is the buy signal?
faq.a4: 100% since 2017. Every single day the model said 'buy', Bitcoin was higher 12 months later. 755 days tested across 30 independent episodes and 3 market cycles.
faq.a5: A strategy that modulates your monthly investment based on the signal zone. It buys more at discounts, sells into strength, and redeploys profits via a 'war chest'. Backtested: +1,118% from $100/month vs +537% for blind DCA.
faq.a6: A risk-adjusted return metric that only penalizes downside volatility (unlike Sharpe which penalizes all volatility). Smart DCA scores 6.58 vs 3.34 for blind DCA — the signal avoids crashes while allowing upside.
faq.a7: No. This is a quantitative research tool. Past accuracy does not guarantee future results. Bitcoin is volatile and the model has known limitations. Always do your own research.
about.title: About
landing.wyg1.title: A clear signal based on probabilities, not opinions
landing.wyg1.desc: The model weighs price position, loss risk, and upside potential across 2,000 simulated scenarios — and gives you one actionable answer: buy, hold, reduce, or get out. No gut feeling, no guesswork.
landing.wyg2.title: A warning before the correction, not after
landing.wyg2.desc: Most tools only tell you when to buy. This one also flags when the price is dangerously stretched above its fair value — so you can reduce before the market does it for you.
landing.wyg3.title: Your real odds of losing money
landing.wyg3.desc: Not a vague 'it might go down.' Exact probabilities at each horizon, computed from 2,000 Monte Carlo simulations calibrated to how Bitcoin actually behaves — fat tails, volatility clustering, and all.
landing.wyg4.title: Backtested against every day since 2017
landing.wyg4.desc: When it said 'buy,' the price was higher 12 months later 100% of the time. When it said 'reduce' or 'sell,' holding would have lost money 72% of the time. No hindsight — only data available at that moment was used.
landing.uth1.title: Power Law — WLS + RANSAC
landing.uth1.desc: Weighted Least Squares on 16 years of daily data with 4-year recency decay. RANSAC for a robust support floor that excludes bubble peaks. EVT/Generalized Pareto Distribution for the empirical upside cap.
landing.uth2.title: Fractal volatility — MMAR
landing.uth2.desc: Mandelbrot's Multifractal Model of Asset Returns with Hurst exponent via DFA and multifractal partition function. Captures fat tails, volatility clustering, and long memory that Gaussian models miss entirely.
landing.uth3.title: Regime detection — OU diagnostic
landing.uth3.desc: Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process with two regimes (calm and volatile). Used as a regime diagnostic — not as the MC engine. The simulation runs on pure MMAR/Hurst dynamics to avoid artificially dampening tail risk.
landing.uth4.title: Monte Carlo — 2,000 paths, 3-year horizon
landing.uth4.desc: Fractal cascades, empirical shock resampling, Hurst-correlated noise. RANSAC reflective floor with empirically calibrated break probability.
landing.uth5.title: Signal calibration — walk-forward backtest
landing.uth5.desc: Buy score weights and sell thresholds calibrated by grid search against historical returns. Probabilistic calibration table shows how well MC loss estimates matched reality across every market cycle.
landing.disclaimer: Past signal accuracy doesn't guarantee future results. Bitcoin is volatile and the model can be wrong. The 100% accuracy figure is historical and based on daily sampling from 2017 to present.
backtest.title: Backtest
backtest.loading: Backtest data not available yet — computing...
backtest.buyQuestion: When the model said "buy", was it right?
backtest.sellQuestion: When the model said "sell", did it protect you?
backtest.subtitle: We tested every single day since 2017 against the real outcome. {nDays} days. Daily sampling. No optimized thresholds.
backtest.daysProfitable12m: {n} out of {n} days profitable at 12 months
backtest.holdLostWithin6m: of the time, holding would have lost money within 6 months
backtest.contextPara: Without the model, if you picked a random day to buy Bitcoin and held 12 months, you'd have lost money **30% of the time**. Our buy signal eliminates that entirely. Our sell signal would have saved you from an average **{avgLoss}% loss** — and in the worst case, **{worst}%**.
backtest.spectrumNote: Buy zones: accuracy = % profitable at 12 months. Sell zones: "saved" = % where holding would have lost money within 6 months. Avg return and worst entry for sell zones show what would have happened if you ignored the signal.
backtest.stabilityPerfect: Perfect stability — same accuracy in every era across 3 market cycles.
backtest.refitNotePerfect: The Power Law is refitted at each test point using only data available up to that date. No future data. The perfect match confirms the signal is not an artifact of look-ahead bias.
backtest.refitNoteNearPerfect: The Power Law is refitted at each test point using only data available up to that date. No future data. The near-perfect match confirms the signal is not an artifact of look-ahead bias.
backtest.threeApproachesPara: If you'd invested $100 every month since 2017, you'd have **{dcaValue}** today. If you'd only invested when our model said "buy", you'd have **{signalValue}** (but most of your budget sat in cash). If you'd followed Smart DCA — buying more at discounts, selling at overheated levels, and redeploying those profits at the next crash — you'd have **{smartValue} from the same {totalBudget} budget**.
backtest.dcaTableNote: Same $100/month budget ({total} total). Cash from skipped months and sell proceeds stays in portfolio. Smart DCA compounds: sell proceeds build a "war chest" that gets redeployed at discounts.
backtest.howSmartDCAPara: Each month, the model tells you how much to invest based on where Bitcoin is relative to fair value. When Bitcoin is cheap, you buy more — including profits from previous sells. When it's expensive, you sell into strength and build a "war chest." Each cycle amplifies the next.
backtest.smartDCANote: War chest = cash from sells + skipped months. In Strong Buy, 30% of the war chest deploys alongside the $100 base. Sell proceeds from 2017 funded bigger buys in 2018. Proceeds from 2021 funded 2022. Each cycle compounds.
backtest.riskParaSimple: Blind DCA lost **{dcaDD}%** peak-to-trough during the worst crash. Smart DCA lost **{smartDD}%** — and was actively buying at those lows with cash from previous sells. Same $100/month budget, dramatically different experience.
backtest.methodology: Methodology: daily sampling (step=1). Buy signal: σ < -0.5. Sell signal: σ > 0.8. DCA strategies use identical $100/month budget with uninvested cash in portfolio. Sortino annualized from monthly portfolio returns (penalizes only downside). Smart DCA compounds sell proceeds into future buy signals via war chest.
pro.note.plMethod: WLS with exponential decay (4-year half-life) · RANSAC for support floor · R² = {r2}
pro.note.mcMethod: 2,000 paths · MMAR/Hurst dynamics · Empirical shock resampling · Reflective floor at RANSAC support
pro.note.plChart: Bitcoin's price has followed a power law growth curve since 2010. The bands show historical deviation ranges. When price touches the upper bands, it tends to correct. When it reaches lower bands, it tends to recover.
pro.note.sigmaChart: How far Bitcoin has deviated from the Power Law model throughout its history. Extreme positive values preceded corrections, extreme negative values preceded rallies.
pro.note.keyLevels: Structurally important price levels from the Power Law model. They act as gravitational anchors across market cycles.
pro.note.forwardProj: Power Law fair value at each horizon with the full σ-band structure. All percentages relative to today's {price}.
pro.note.riskMatrix: Power Law statistical distribution vs Monte Carlo simulation at each percentile (1Y horizon).
pro.note.plLogScale: Log-log scale · X: log₁₀(days since genesis) · Y: log₁₀(price USD)
pro.driver.holdNeutral: Neutral — odds favor you (83%) but downside appears
pro.driver.hurstDiagTitle: Momentum diagnostics (Hurst) — informational, not used for signal
pro.driver.hurstDiagDesc: Hurst divergences measure trend persistence breakdown. Shown for context — the sell signal uses σ thresholds only, validated out-of-sample.
pro.driver.howToRead: Buy and sell signals use pure σ thresholds validated by walk-forward backtest with local Power Law refit at each point. No weight optimization, no in-sample calibration. Buy at σ < -0.5 has 100% historical accuracy. Sell at σ > 0.8 has 0% accuracy at 12 months. MC probabilities are shown as context — they inform the narrative but don't drive the signal.
pro.hurst.combo.strongAccumDesc: Undervalued with persistent upward trend. Historically the best entry zone.
pro.hurst.combo.choppyDiscountDesc: Below fair value but no clear trend. Good for patient DCA.
pro.hurst.combo.quietDiscountDesc: Undervalued in a neutral trend. Accumulation is reasonable.
pro.hurst.combo.momentumRallyDesc: Above fair value with persistent trend. Can run further but risk is elevated.
pro.hurst.combo.exhaustionDesc: Overvalued and losing momentum. Historically precedes corrections.
pro.hurst.combo.elevatedDesc: Above fair value, trend neutral. Caution warranted.
pro.hurst.combo.healthyTrendDesc: Near fair value with momentum. A constructive environment.
pro.hurst.combo.consolidationDesc: Range-bound near fair value. No strong edge either way.
pro.hurst.combo.neutralDesc: No strong signal from the σ + Hurst combination.
pro.hurst.vol.silentAccumDesc: Volatility compressing while trend persists. Classic institutional footprint.
pro.hurst.vol.preBreakoutDesc: Low volatility, no trend. The spring is coiling.
pro.hurst.vol.quietCompressionDesc: Volatility dropping, trend neutral. Something is building.
pro.hurst.vol.trendAccelDesc: Volatility expanding with trend. Late-stage momentum.
pro.hurst.vol.chaoticReversalDesc: Volatility spiking with no direction. High risk.
pro.hurst.vol.volSpikeDesc: Vol expanding but no clear trend. Reduce position size.
pro.hurst.vol.normalDesc: No vol compression or expansion, no strong trend.
pro.regime.bottomNote: Market regime is determined by two axes: position (σ from Power Law, validated by backtest) and direction (momentum autocorrelation). Hurst, volatility, and mean-reversion speed are shown as supporting diagnostics — they inform the narrative but don't determine the regime.
pro.episode.atFairValue: At fair value — no active episode.
mc.heading: Monte Carlo
mc.intro: You buy Bitcoin today at {price}. We simulate 2,000 possible outcomes over 3 years using a proprietary model built for how Bitcoin actually behaves — crashes, rallies, and the long silences in between. The result is a probability map of what could happen and how likely each scenario is.
mc.evenInWorst: Even in the worst 5% of scenarios, you're in profit.
mc.lossChance: There's a {pct}% chance you lose money.
mc.scenarioBecomes: In the most likely scenario, your {price} becomes
mc.howToReadDesc: The bright line in the middle is the median — the outcome that's equally likely to be above or below. The shaded green area above it covers the top 50% of scenarios. The shaded red area below covers the bottom 50%. The faint dashed lines at the edges are the extremes — only 5% of simulations ended up beyond them. The wider the fan, the more uncertainty. Notice how it narrows relative to the median over time — that's the Power Law anchor pulling outcomes toward structural fair value.
mc.ourModelDesc: Standard Monte Carlo assumes returns follow a bell curve — symmetric, thin-tailed, no memory. Bitcoin's returns don't work like that. Our model uses Mandelbrot's Multifractal Model of Asset Returns (MMAR): fractal cascades generate volatility clustering, the Hurst exponent captures long memory in trends, and empirical shock resampling preserves the actual shape of Bitcoin's tail risk. The simulation respects the Power Law support floor with a calibrated break probability. The result: 2,000 paths that behave like Bitcoin actually behaves, not like a textbook.
pl.heading: Power Law
footer.tech: Santostasi Power Law · Mandelbrot MMAR · DFA · Regime-switching · EVT/GPD · Walk-forward backtest · Spot refreshes 60s · Browser-only
